name: Malicious Insider — Privileged Employee Data Theft
description: A malicious insider (departing senior engineer with privileged access) systematically exfiltrates intellectual property, customer PII, and trade secrets over several weeks before resignation. The attack uses legitimate tools and authorized access to evade detection, including off-hours access, personal cloud storage upload, encrypted archive creation, and USB exfiltration.

phases.name: Off-Hours Sensitive File Access
phases.description: The insider accesses sensitive files (R&D blueprints, customer database, financial projections) outside normal business hours (02:30 AM), using their legitimate credentials and access rights. Volume is 5x normal usage.

phases.name: Bulk File Copy to Local Staging Directory
phases.description: The insider uses robocopy and xcopy to stage gigabytes of sensitive data in a hidden local directory, preparing for exfiltration via multiple channels.

phases.name: Archive Creation with Password Encryption
phases.description: The staged data is compressed into multiple password-protected 7-Zip archives, split into 500MB chunks to avoid detection by DLP solutions that monitor large file transfers.
phases.technique_name: Archive Collected Data: Archive via Utility

phases.name: Exfiltration to Personal Cloud Storage
phases.description: Archives are uploaded to the insider's personal Google Drive, OneDrive, and Mega accounts using browser-based upload and rclone, bypassing DLP rules that only monitor email attachments.
phases.technique_name: Exfiltration Over Web Service: Exfiltration to Cloud Storage

phases.name: USB Device Exfiltration
phases.description: A personal USB drive is connected and the staged data is also copied there as a redundant exfiltration channel. The USB device is not registered in the corporate device inventory.
phases.technique_name: Exfiltration Over Physical Medium: Exfiltration over USB

phases.name: Log Tampering to Cover Tracks
phases.description: Before leaving, the insider uses their admin rights to clear Windows Event Logs, PowerShell history, and browser history. They also modify the access timestamps of the copied files using BulkFileChanger.
phases.technique_name: Indicator Removal: Clear Windows Event Logs
